revision.text:
[[en:Ajisukitakahikone]][[es:Ajisukitakahikone]][[fr:Ajisukitakahikone]][[hu:Adzsiszukitakahikone]][[it:Ajisukitakahikone]][[ja:アヂスキタカヒコネ]][[mni:ꯑꯖꯤꯁꯨꯀꯤꯇꯀꯍꯤꯀꯣꯅꯦ]][[ru:Адзи-суки-така-хико-нэ]][[simple:Ajisukitakahikone]][[zh:味耜高彥根神]] {{Short description|Japanese deity|qq=}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Ajisukitakahikone-no-Kami | image = Ajisukitakahikone.jpg | caption = Ajisukitakahikone destroys the hut where [[Ame no Wakahiko|Ame-no-Wakahiko]]'s corpse lay with his sword | god_of = God of agriculture and thunder | script_name = Japanese | script = 味耜高彦根神 | other_names = Ajishikitakahikone-no-Kami (阿遅志貴高日子根神, 阿遅志貴高日子根神, 阿治志貴高日子根神)
Ajisukitakahiko-no-Mikoto (阿遅須枳高日子命)
Ajisukitakahikone-no-Mikoto (味耜高彦根命, 阿遅須伎高孫根乃命, 味鉏高彦根尊)
Takakamo-Ajisukitakahikone-no-Mikoto (高鴨阿治須岐託彦根命)
Kamo-no-Ōmikami (迦毛之大御神) | cult_center = {{ill|Takakamo Shrine|ja|高鴨神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakamo Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakamo Shrine|1=Takakamo Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11673295}}, Asuki Shrine | symbols = | consort = Ame-no-Mikajihime | parents =[[Ōkuninushi]] and {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}; {{ill|Takiribime|qid=Q10932870|1=Tagorihime}}| siblings = {{ill|Shitateruhime|ja|下照比売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シタテルヒメ}}
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]] and others (half-siblings) | children = {{ill|Takitsuhiko|en|Takitsuhiko|qid=Q96407698|lt=Takitsuhiko|1=Takitsuhiko}}, Yamuyabiko | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', ''[[:en:Sendai Kuji Hongi|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'', {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} ''[[Fudoki]]'', {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|qid=Q1047523|lt=Harima|1=Harima Province}} ''Fudoki'' | gender = {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|qid=Q10940674|1=Ame no Wakahiko}}; {{ill|Honoakari|qid=Q60673696|1=Honoakari}}; {{ill|Honosusori|qid=Q10731395|1=Hosuseri}}; {{ill|Nigihayahi|qid=Q10949054|1=Nigihayahi}} }} '''Ajisukitakahikone''' (also '''Ajishikitakahikone''' or '''Ajisukitakahiko''') is a ''[[kami]]'' in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is one of the sons of [[Ōkuninushi]] and the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|qid=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity|1=Tutelary deity}} of {{ill|Kamo, Niigata|en|Kamo, Niigata|qid=Q857293|lt=Kamo|1=Kamo, Niigata}}.{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese mythology |date=2008 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-533262-9 |series=Handbooks of world mythology |location=Oxford |pages=110-111|qq=}} ==Name== The god is referred to both as 'Ajisukitakahikone-no-Kami' (阿遅鉏高日子根神; {{ill|Old Japanese|en|Old Japanese|qid=Q5736700|lt=Old Japanese|1=Old Japanese}}: ''Adisuki1takapi1ko1ne-no2-Kami2'') and 'Ajishikitakahikone-no-Kami' (阿遅志貴高日子根神; {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|qid=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana|1=Man'yōgana}}: 阿治志貴多迦比古泥能迦微; O.J.: ''Adisiki2takapi1ko1ne''{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |page=450|qq=}}) in the ''[[Kojiki]]'',{{cite wikisource |language=zh|wslink=zh:古事記/上卷|title=古事記 上卷|qq=}} while the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' consistently calls him 'Ajisukitakahikone-no-Kami' (味耜高彥根神).{{cite wikisource |language=zh|wslink=zh:日本書紀/卷第二|title=日本書紀 卷第二 神代下|qq=}} Renditions of the name found in other texts include 'Ajisukitakahiko-no-Mikoto' (阿遅須枳高日子命; {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} ''[[Fudoki]]''), 'Ajisukitakahikone-no-Mikoto-no-Kami' (阿遅須伎高日古尼命神; {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|qid=Q1047523|lt=Harima|1=Harima Province}} ''Fudoki'') and 'Ajisukitakahikone-no-Mikoto' (阿遅須伎高孫根乃命; ''Izumo-no-[[Kuni no miyatsuko|Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]] no Kan'yogoto'').{{cite web |title=阿遅志貴高日子根神 |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/ajishikitakahikonenokami/ |website=古事記学センター |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-10-25|qq=}} ''Aji'' (O.J. ''adi'') may mean either "excellent" (cf. ''[[:wikt:味#Etymology 1|aji]]'' "taste, flavor") or "flock, mass, many", while ''shiki'' (O.J. ''siki2'') is variously interpreted either as a corruption of ''suki'' (O.J. ''suki1'', "{{ill|Spade|en|Spade|qid=Q216642|lt=Spade|1=Spade}}" or "{{ill|Plough|en|Plough|qid=Q11464|lt=Plough|1=Plough}}"), a derivation from the [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] {{ill|Baekje language|en|Baekje language|qid=Q4841264|lt=word|1=Baekje language}} ''suki'' "village", a word meaning "blade", or a place name in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}. (One factor that complicates a proper interpretation of the name is that ''ki1'' and ''ki2'' [[Old Japanese#Syllables|are thought to be different syllables]] in Old Japanese.) {{ill|Basil Hall Chamberlain|en|Basil Hall Chamberlain|qid=Q810029|lt=Basil Hall Chamberlain|1=Basil Hall Chamberlain}}, in his 1882 translation of the ''Kojiki'', left the name untranslated (noting that "[t]he meaning of the first two members of this compound name is altogether obscure");Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj033.htm#fn_538 Section XXVI.—The Deities the August Descendants of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.] likewise, [[William George Aston]] (1896) merely commented that there is "no satisfactory explanation of this name."{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=67|qq=}} [[Don Philippi|Donald Philippi]] (1968) proposed two possible interpretations of the name: "Massed-Ploughs High-Princeling Deity" or "Excellent Shiki High-Princeling Deity" (with 'Shiki' being understood here to be a toponym). Gustav Heldt's translation of the ''Kojiki'' (2014) meanwhile renders the name as "Lofty Little Lad of Fine Plows".{{cite book |last1=Heldt |first1=Gustav |title=The Kojiki: An Account of Ancient Matters |date=2014 |publisher=Columbia University Press |isbn=978-0231163880 |pages=38, 44|qq=}} ==Mythology== ===Parentage=== The ''Kojiki'' describes Ajisukitakahikone as one of the two children of the god [[Ōkuninushi]] by Tagorihime, one of the three [[Munakata Taisha|Munakata goddesses]], the other being {{ill|Shitateruhime|ja|下照比売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シタテルヒメ}} (also known as Takahime).{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |page=113|qq=}} He is frequently portrayed as a baby who is unable to sleep. His mother carried him up and down a ladder in an attempt to make him sleep, this is what causes the sound of growing thunder. In infancy, his crying and screaming were so loud that he had to be placed in a {{ill|Boat|en|Boat|qid=Q35872|lt=Boat|1=Boat}} and sailed around the islands of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} until he was calm. In adulthood, he was the father of {{ill|Takitsuhiko|en|Takitsuhiko|qid=Q96407698|lt=Takitsuhiko|1=Takitsuhiko}}, a rain god. ===Ajisukitakahikone and Ame-no-Wakahiko=== {{see also|Kuni-yuzuri|qq=}} When the sun goddess[[Amaterasu]] and the [[Kotoamatsukami|primordial god]] [[Takamimusubi]], the rulers of the heavenly realm of [[Takamagahara]], decreed that the earth below ([[:en:Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni|Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni]]) should be ruled over by Amaterasu's progeny, they dispatched a series of messengers to its ruler, Ōkuninushi, to command him to cede supremacy over the land. One of these, [[Ame no Wakahiko|Ame-no-Wakahiko]], ended up marrying {{ill|Shitateruhime|ja|下照比売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シタテルヒメ}}, one of Ōkuninushi's daughters, and even plotted to gain the land for himself. After eight years had passed, a {{ill|Pheasant|en|Pheasant|qid=Q214169|lt=Pheasant|1=Pheasant}} sent by the heavenly gods arrived and remonstrated with Ame-no-Wakahiko, who killed it with his bow and arrow. The arrow flew up to Takamagahara, but was then promptly thrown back to earth; it struck Ame-no-Wakahiko in the chest while he was asleep, killing him instantly. During Ame-no-Wakahiko's funeral, {{ill|Shitateruhime|ja|下照比売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シタテルヒメ}}'s brother Ajisukitakahikone, a close friend of Ame-no-Wakahiko, arrived to pay his condolences. As he closely resembled Ame-no-Wakahiko in appearance, the family of the deceased mistook him for Ame-no-Wakahiko come back to life. Offended at being mistaken for his friend (as corpses were regarded as [[Kegare|unclean]], to be compared with or mistaken for a dead person was seen as an insult), Ajisukitakahikone in anger drew his [[Totsuka-no-Tsurugi|ten-span sword]], hacked to pieces the funeral hut (喪屋 ''moya'') where Ame-no-Wakahiko's corpse was laid and the funeral held, and then kicked it away. The ruined hut landed in the {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|qid=Q864156|lt=land of Mino|1=Mino Province}} and became a mountain called Moyama (喪山, lit. 'mourning mountain').{{efn|Two locations in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|qid=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture|1=Gifu Prefecture}} (the southern part of which is the historical {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|qid=Q864156|lt=province of Mino|1=Mino Province}}) have been suggested as possible candidates for this mountain or hill: a [[Kofun|tumulus]] known as Moyama Kofun (喪山古墳) in {{ill|Tarui, Gifu|en|Tarui, Gifu|qid=Q1345531|lt=Tarui|1=Tarui, Gifu}}, {{ill|Fuwa District, Gifu|en|Fuwa District, Gifu|qid=Q521316|lt=Fuwa District|1=Fuwa District, Gifu}},{{cite web |title=喪山（その2） |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/chimei/moyama-2/ |website=古事記学センター |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-10-27|qq=}} and Moyama Tenjin Shrine (喪山天神社, ''Moyama-Tenjinsha'') in Ōyada, {{ill|Mino, Gifu|en|Mino, Gifu|qid=Q853835|lt=Mino City|1=Mino, Gifu}}.{{cite web |title=喪山（その1） |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/chimei/moyama/ |website=古事記学センター |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-10-27|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Yamamoto |first1=Akira |title=Ichiban yasashii Kojiki no hon (いちばんやさしい古事記の本) |date=2012 |publisher=Seitōsha |page=85 |isbn=9784791620609 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mfKQMtbM6aUC&pg=PA85|qq=}}|qq=}} Ajishikitakahikone, still fuming, then flew off, the radiance that exuded from him being such that it illuminated the space of two hills and two valleys. {{ill|Shitateruhime|ja|下照比売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シタテルヒメ}}, wishing to reveal to the mourners her brother's identity, then composed the following song in his honor:{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=123–128|qq=}}{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=65-67, 73-75|qq=}} :{| ! {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|qid=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana|1=Man'yōgana}} (''Kojiki'')|| || Japanese|| || {{ill|Old Japanese|en|Old Japanese|qid=Q5736700|lt=Old Japanese|1=Old Japanese}} || || Modern Japanese ([[:en:Rōmaji|Rōmaji]]) || || Translated by Donald Philippi |- | 阿米那流夜
淤登多那婆多能
宇那賀世流
多麻能美須麻流
美須麻流能
阿那陀麻波夜
美多邇
布多和多良須
阿治志貴
多迦比古泥
能迦微曾{{cite book |last1=Takeda |first1=Yūkichi |title=記紀歌謡集 (Kiki Kayōshu) |date=1948 |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |page=17 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1069683/10|qq=}} |   | {{lang|ja|天なるや
弟棚機の
うながせる
玉の御統
御統に
あな玉はや
み谷
二渡らす
阿遅志貴
高日子根の
神ぞ{{cite web |author1=Takeda, Yūkichi |title=古事記 (Kojiki) |url=https://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/001518/files/51731_50813.html |website=Aozora Bunko |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |access-date=2020-10-30 |date=1956|qq=}}|qq=}} |   | ''Ame[[Old Japanese#Syllables|2]] naru ya
Oto2-tanabata no2
unagaseru
tama no2 misumaru
misumaru ni
anadama pa ya
mi1tani
puta watarasu
Adisiki2
Takapi1ko1ne no2
Kami2 so2''Based on {{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |year=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=428|qq=}} The transcription system used to distinguish type A/B vowels in the original text had been changed to [[Old Japanese#Transcription|indexed notation]]. |   | ''Ame naru ya
Oto-tanabata no
unagaseru
tama no misumaru
misumaru ni
anadama ha ya
mitani
futa watarasu
Ajishiki
Takahikone no
Kami zo'' |   | Ah, the large jewel{{efn|Literally "hole-jewel" (''anadama'')|qq=}}
Strung on the cord of beads
Worn around the neck
Of the heavenly
Young weaving maiden!
Like this is he
Who crosses
Two valleys at once,
The god Ajishiki-
Takahikone! |} ==Notes== {{notelist|qq=}} ==References== {{reflist|qq=}} ==See also== *[[Ōkuninushi]] *[[Takemikazuchi]] *[[Ame no Wakahiko]] {{jmyth navbox long|qq=}} {{Kamo Faith|qq=}} {{Shizuri Faith|qq=}} [[en:Ajisukitakahikone]] [[en:Ajisukitakahikone]] [[es:Ajisukitakahikone]] [[fr:Ajisukitakahikone]] [[hu:Adzsiszukitakahikone]] [[it:Ajisukitakahikone]] [[ja:アヂスキタカヒコネ]] [[mni:ꯑꯖꯤꯁꯨꯀꯤꯇꯀꯍꯤꯀꯣꯅꯦ]] [[ru:Адзи-суки-така-хико-нэ]] [[simple:Ajisukitakahikone]] [[zh:味耜高彥根神]] {{wikidata link|Q506610}} [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Agricultural deities]] [[Category:Thunder deities]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]]
